nativeRange.habitat: Lagoon and inshore coral reefs, particularly in areas where silting is prevalent; generally found at depths of 1–10 m. Adults have been documented aggregating over coral outcrops in shallow reef environments.

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented aggregating over coral outcrops to feed on zooplankton. In captivity, generally accepts small crustaceans such as mysis, brine shrimp, and krill, along with zooplankton-based foods such as cyclops and copepods; frozen and prepared marine foods are commonly accepted.

avoidWith: other similar-looking damsels in small tanks

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Debra Baker (1875x1352). Sharpest, most vibrant single specimen; clearly shows the diagnostic blue iridescent spot-rows along a green/lavender body, yellow eye-ring and dark pectoral-base spot on a live in-colour fish.

Photo by Debra Baker (1508x1344). Multiple individuals (shoaling species); shows the striking elongated yellow soft-dorsal/caudal filaments and blue spotting well. Slightly soft underwater haze, so kept as non-recommended.
Photo by Debra Baker (1666x2048). Two fish with dramatic trailing yellow dorsal/anal/caudal filaments and blue body speckling; good group/behaviour shot. Mild underwater haze, non-recommended.

summary: A small Western Pacific damselfish found in shallow, silty reef lagoons. Generally considered reef-safe and hardy; tends to be territorial toward similar species but is commonly kept by beginner and experienced hobbyists alike.
careNotes:
The Coral Demoiselle (Neopomacentrus nemurus) is a small damselfish that reaches approximately 8 cm in total length. It is native to shallow lagoon and inshore reef habitats across the Western Central Pacific, with populations documented in Indonesia, the Philippines, Palau, Papua New Guinea, the Solomon Islands, and Vanuatu, typically at depths of 1 to 10 m. The species is generally considered hardy and reef-safe, and most aquarium sources note that it will not bother corals or most invertebrates.

The Coral Demoiselle tends to be territorial, particularly toward conspecifics (members of its own species) and similar-looking damselfish. Simultaneous introduction of any conspecifics and a sufficiently spacious tank are commonly advised to reduce aggression. Providing ample live rock and coral structure gives individuals places to retreat, which tends to lower stress-related territorial behavior.

In the wild, this species feeds by aggregating over coral outcrops to pick zooplankton from the water column. In captivity it has been reported to readily accept a variety of small frozen and prepared foods, including mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, krill, cyclops, and copepods.
breedingNotes: Oviparous with distinct pairing reported. Eggs are demersal and adhere to the substrate; males have been documented guarding and aerating the eggs. Captive breeding is considered possible but is not commonly reported among hobbyists.

primary.name: FishBase
primary.notes: Used as primary source after LiveAquaria collection page (https://www.liveaquaria.com/collections/damselfish) did not carry a listing for Neopomacentrus nemurus. FishBase provided taxonomy, max size (8.0 cm), native range (Western Central Pacific), temperature (25–29.3°C), depth range (1–10 m), habitat description, diet (zooplankton), breeding biology (oviparous, male egg-guard), and IUCN Least Concern status.

additional.name: fishy.wiki
additional.notes: Secondary source with species-specific aquarium care data. Confirmed adult size (8.0 cm), reef-safe status, tank minimum (200 L), temperature range (22.2–25.6°C), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (SG 1.020–1.025), KH (8–12), diet (mysis, brine shrimp, krill, zooplankton), and beginner-suitable hardiness. Temperament noted as aggressive toward similar species. Temperature disagreement with FishBase wild data documented in careNotes.

additional.name: LiveAquaria
additional.notes: Designated primary source (per manifest); no listing found for Neopomacentrus nemurus on the damselfish collection page at time of access. Role swapped to FishBase as primary per playbook fallback rule.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous with male nest-guarding of demersal adhesive eggs. Captive breeding is reported as possible but is not commonly achieved by hobbyists.
